creator: dhekir@gmail.com
text:
I stumbled upon a very unintuitive behavior of diff: when option -q is used, -B is ignored, unless it is also accompanied by -b, as in the following examples

# create test files: one containing a single newline, another containing 2 newlines
$ echo "" > a
$ echo -e "\n" > b

$ diff a b -B # ok: no diff reported
$ diff a b -B -q # weird: -B seems ignored, diff reported (GNU diff does not report it)
$ diff a b -b # ok: -b only ignores spaces, not entire lines
$ diff a b -b -q # ok: the diff remains, -q does not affect it
$ diff a b -B -b -q # ok: this time, -B was taken into account

So, we have:

1. -B works on its own;
2. when -B -q is used, -B seems ignored;
3. -b on its own does not suffice to remove the diff in these files
4. -B -b -q works (no diff), but since -b is irrelevant without -q, this seems like a bug.

In GNU diff, `diff a b -B -q` behaves as what I consider "expected": the files are considered equal.

If this is intended behavior, the documentation needs to mention it.

Bug #252515 is similar to this one (it mentioned `-w`, while this one mentions `-B`).
